message.content:
## Web Searcher: training and adaptation in blind listeners

Research question: "Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible."

Your angle: **training and adaptation in blind listeners** — The above-16x claim comes from blind screen-reader users; finds studies on training and cortical adaptation.
Search query: `blind listeners ultra-fast speech screen reader adaptation training 16x time-compressed comprehension neuroplasticity`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
Web search results for query: "blind ultrafast speech comprehension visual cortex recruitment fMRI time-compressed speech"

Links: [{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study | BMC Neuroscience | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74"},{"title":"Network Modeling for Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) Signals during Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Late-Blind Listeners | PLOS One","url":"https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0132196"},{"title":"Frontiers | How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?","url":"https://frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00530/full"},{"title":"How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects? - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3745084/"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24167485/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects? - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23966968/"}]

The web search returned comprehensive results on this specialized neuroscience topic. Here's what the research shows:

## Key Findings

Individuals with vision loss can learn to understand spoken language at rates up to about 22 syllables per second, far exceeding the maximum performance of sighted listeners (approximately 8 syllables per second).

### Brain Activation Patterns

An fMRI study examined blind subjects of varying ultra-fast speech comprehension abilities while listening to sentence utterances at moderately fast (8 syllables/second) or ultra-fast (16 syllables/second) rates, finding that blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar, in addition to language-related areas like the left inferior frontal gyrus and bilateral posterior superior temporal sulcus.

### Visual Cortex Role

The visual cortex appears to be an essential prerequisite to enhanced speech encoding at high syllable rates. Interestingly, sighted subjects appear unable or at least not to "attempt" to recruit the central-visual system to speed up comprehension of spoken language.

### Proposed Mechanism

Blind subjects seem to recruit part of their visual cortex via subcortical afferent auditory information to represent prosodic information and transfer it as an event-trigger channel to the frontal part of the speech processing network.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "blind listeners ultra-fast speech screen reader adaptation training 16x time-compressed comprehension neuroplasticity"

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) Why do blind listeners use visual cortex for understanding ultra‐fast speech?","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/224881917_Why_do_blind_listeners_use_visual_cortex_for_understanding_ultra-fast_speech"},{"title":"Comprehension of ultra-fast speech-blind vs.\" normally hearing\" persons | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228846770_Comprehension_of_ultra-fast_speech-blind_vs_normally_hearing_persons"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects? - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3745084/"},{"title":"How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects?","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3745084/"},{"title":"Frontiers | Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/"},{"title":"On-line plasticity in spoken sentence comprehension: Adapting to time-compressed speech","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2775905/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study | BMC Neuroscience | Springer Nature Link","url":"http://www.biomedcentral.com/1471-2202/14/74"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24167485/"}]

The search has returned highly relevant research on this topic. Here are the key findings:

## Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Blind Listeners

Blind subjects can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for "reading" texts at ultra-fast speaking rates (>16 syllables/s), exceeding by far the normal range of 6 syllables/s.

## Screen Reader Adaptation and Training

Screen reader accessibility studies that measured listening rate by accuracy at answering questions spoken by a screen reader at various rates found that people who are visually impaired, who often rely on audio cues and access text aurally, generally have higher listening rates than sighted people.

## Neural Reorganization and Neuroplasticity

Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) documented significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1) and contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG) in a blind university student with high ultra-fast speech perception capabilities during application of compressed verbal utterances (16 syl/s).

A stronger engagement of classical language network components can help speed up encoding processes, providing an example of neuroplasticity due to redistribution of brain activity.

## Mechanisms of Enhancement

The visual system can impact auditory perception at basic computational stages such as temporal signal resolution, and blind as compared to sighted subjects are more resistant against backward masking, with this ability appearing to be associated with visual cortex activity.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: I have strong results. Let me do one more targeted search on the training/adaptation aspect and the specific 16x+ claim.

input.query: screen reader users listening speed words per minute training adaptation accelerated text-to-speech blind 800 wpm

content.content:
Web search results for query: "screen reader users listening speed words per minute training adaptation accelerated text-to-speech blind 800 wpm"

Links: [{"title":"The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users","url":"https://vuink.com/post/fghss-d-dvagreserr-d-dpn/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders-d-dhtml"},{"title":"Software Development at 800 Words Per Minute | Dickson Tan's blog","url":"https://neurrone.com/posts/software-development-at-800-wpm/"},{"title":"screen reader and Speech output - why is it so fast and sounds artificial? - Accessibility Consulting - Training & Support","url":"https://www.netz-barrierefrei.de/en/screenreader-output.html"},{"title":"Sam's Stuff - The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users","url":"https://stuff.interfree.ca/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders.html"},{"title":"Comprehension of ultra-fast speech-blind vs.\" normally hearing\" persons | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228846770_Comprehension_of_ultra-fast_speech-blind_vs_normally_hearing_persons"},{"title":"The Landscape of Fast Reading: Science, Technology, Education, and Field Applications – ERIC KIM","url":"https://erickimphotography.com/the-landscape-of-fast-reading-science-technology-education-and-field-applications/"},{"title":"Text Platform | Screen readers: hearing the unseen","url":"https://platform.text.com/resource-center/updates/livechat-fast-screen-readers"}]

I've completed a web search on screen reader users' listening speeds and text-to-speech technology. Here are the key findings:

## Listening Speeds for Screen Reader Users

Blind users prefer voices that can be understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute, for comparison.

## Training and Adaptation

Unlike human speech, a screen reader's synthetic voice reads a word in the same way every time, making it possible to get used to how it speaks. At first, it requires conscious effort to understand. With years of practice, comprehension becomes automatic. People who are visually impaired, who often rely on audio cues and access text aurally, generally have higher listening rates than sighted people.

## Voice Preferences

Blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient. The rate that screen reader users absorb information is limited by how fast the screen reader is speaking, so developing the ability to understand it at higher speech rates is critical.

The research indicates that experienced screen reader users develop the cognitive ability to comprehend accelerated synthetic speech at rates far exceeding normal human speech through consistent practice and exposure.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: I have comprehensive results across the neuroscience studies, training/adaptation, and the firsthand screen-reader-user accounts. Let me compile the most relevant findings.

results.title: Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans (Dietrich et al., Frontiers in Human Neuroscience 2013)
results.snippet: The single most directly on-point source for the 'training/adaptation' angle. Late-blind subjects underwent a structured training program in ultra-fast speech comprehension; pre/post fMRI showed training itself induced functional reorganization recruiting the central-visual system (V1, fusiform gyrus). Demonstrates causally that the >16 syl/s capacity is a learned, neuroplastic adaptation rather than an innate trait, and that comprehension improves with practice. Directly explains why blind screen-reader users reach playback rates sighted listeners cannot.

results.title: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar - an fMRI study (Dietrich et al., BMC Neuroscience 2013)
results.snippet: Establishes the quantitative perceptual limit and the neural substrate. Blind proficient listeners comprehend speech at >16 syllables/s (up to ~22 syl/s) versus a sighted ceiling near 8 syl/s. fMRI at 8 vs 16 syl/s shows blind experts uniquely activate right V1, contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar alongside the classical left-IFG/STS language network. Provides the cortical-recruitment mechanism behind extreme accelerated-speech intelligibility and the 16x-class claim.

results.title: How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects? (Hertrich et al., Frontiers in Psychology 2013)
results.snippet: Goes beyond localization to the signal-processing/perceptual mechanism. Argues blind listeners route subcortical auditory (pulvinar) information into visual cortex to represent prosody as an 'event-trigger' channel that segments and times the fast stream, and that visual-cortex engagement confers greater resistance to backward masking - i.e. enhanced temporal resolution. Bridges the neuroscience to why prosody/timing processing is what makes extreme time-compressed speech parseable.

results.title: Software Development at 800 Words Per Minute (Dickson Tan)
results.snippet: Firsthand practitioner account from a blind screen-reader user, grounding the academic claims in real usage. Explains that synthetic voices read each word identically every time, so users 'get used to' the voice; comprehension that initially requires conscious effort becomes automatic after years of practice. Concretely documents the 800-900 wpm regime (vs ~200 wpm normal speech) and frames it explicitly as a trained adaptation - the experiential counterpart to the cortical-reorganization studies.

results.title: On-line plasticity in spoken sentence comprehension: Adapting to time-compressed speech (Peelle & Wingfield 2005)
results.snippet: Addresses short-timescale (within-session) adaptation in the general population, complementing the long-term blind-expert literature. Shows even sighted listeners rapidly improve comprehension of time-compressed speech with brief exposure, and that adaptation generalizes across compression rates - establishing that rapid perceptual learning of accelerated speech is a baseline human capacity that blind users extend through extensive training.

results.title: Network Modeling for fMRI Signals during Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Late-Blind Listeners (Dietrich/Hertrich et al., PLOS ONE 2015)
results.snippet: Effective-connectivity (dynamic causal modeling) analysis of how the recruited regions interact during ultra-fast comprehension in late-blind listeners. Adds mechanistic depth on the directional information flow (pulvinar to V1 to frontal language areas) underlying the adapted network, supporting the redistribution-of-brain-activity account of how extreme speed-ups become intelligible.

content.text: I've completed the search and returned the top 6 results via StructuredOutput. The findings cover the full arc of the training/adaptation angle: the causal training study (Dietrich 2013 Frontiers), the foundational fMRI localization study establishing the >16 syl/s limit and visual-cortex recruitment, the audiovisual-pathway mechanism paper, a firsthand 800-wpm screen-reader-user account, the on-line plasticity baseline study, and the connectivity-modeling follow-up.
